content:
So @Brian Teague brought this up, and I think it is likely how we should handle Default Valued Record Fields.

Simply put, a user can add a default value to any field of a struct in the type definition. Ignore the exact syntax, but something like:

Point : { x: F32, y: F32, z: F32 = 0.0 }

The reads as: We have a type Point. It contains the floats: x, y, and z. z has a default value of 0.0.

I think this fixes the inconsistencies and confusion around optional record fields while also being a more generally useful concept.

For starters, throw a roc at any systems level programming language (and many higher level ones), and they have this feature. Default initialization is generally useful. It becomes exceptionally powerful with configuration structs where users will mostly use the default initialization of fields, but might override the initialization of a few specific fields.

Rust:

Foo {
    bar: "bar".to_string(),
    baz: 42,
    ..Default::default()
}

Zig:

const Foo = struct {
    a: i32 = 1234,
    b: i32,
};

C/C++:

struct foo {
    bool a = true;
    bool b = true;
    bool c;
 } bar;

I think that this:

  1. Changes the name to default record field instead of optional record field which will clear the common beginner confusion.
  2. It no longer requires destructuring the record.
  3. It is more flexible and powerful.
  4. Still only gives the power of default values with names.

Downsides:

  1. Requires a type annotation to use. That said, it will never break our type decidablility. People will just have to explicitly fill out all types if an annotation doesn't exist.

List.range would become:

List.range : {
    start : [At (Num a), After (Num a)],
    end : [At (Num a), Before (Num a), Length (Num a)],
    step : Num a = 0,
} -> ...
This is clearer, showing what the default value actually is in the type.
so this would make it no longer true that you can delete all the type annotations in a Roc program and it will still type-check?
because I can call the function leaving off certain fields thanks to the annotation, but then if the annotation gets commented out, now I'm missing fields and get a type mismatch
Correct, but I think it is the way we should go.

To use this feature, you do require type annoations, but it is also trivial (but verbose) to remove the features. If you want to delete all type annotations in a program (no idea why you would do this), simply fill in all of the default values while deleting the type annotations

Also, it is an additive feature. If someone never uses type annotations, they simply will never use this feature. So no real cost to the people who don't use type annotations, they are just skipping a single feature in the language.

you can delete all the type annotations in a Roc program and it will still type-check

I personally think this isn't a feature we should focus on keeping around. What value does it give us.

I think this would also solve the problem that coming from other language you might abuse the optional fields, as I've done in https://roc.zulipchat.com/#narrow/stream/231634-beginners/topic/Compiler.20says.20optional.20record.20fields.20are.20missing/near/423171715.

With this, it's really just setting defaults so you don't need to set them all manually, not something you could mistake for optionality.
I think it is important that a program can be written without ever annotating types, but I don't think this inverse removal piece is important.

Brendan Hansknecht said:

I think it is important that a program can be written without ever annotating types, but I don't think this inverse removal piece is important.

This feels weird, somehow, but I can't think of a counterargument. :D

you can delete all the type annotations in a Roc program and it will still type-check

Wait, with abilities, this already isn't true, right?
nope, abilities preserve it
Yeah, if remove the type annotation from Dict, it will no longer implement Eq. So any use of == will break
Dict is a type though :big_smile:
but to be fair, the main benefit is being able to write things in an implementation-first style if you want to
which this would only affect if you wanted to use that style when writing a function that would take a record that has default values
but it also means Roc couldn't reasonably claim to have principal decidable type inference anymore

# X := U32 implements [ Eq ]
main =
    if @X 1 == @X 2 then
        Stdout.line "Eq"
    else
        Stdout.line "Not Eq"
which might be okay, but I definitely am not a fan of giving that up for what feels mostly like a small adjustment to the way the current feature works
This is your broken example by abilities
that doesn't compile unless you define X
You said that you should be able to comment out any types and roc should still compile
type annotations
like the line above the implementation of a function or constant
Interesting. So this is just a type alias problem.
I mean opaque types are a type-level feature, if you're reaching for them it's because you specifically want your implementation to be based on types
yeah exactly
Couldn't you say the same about default record fields?
today they work fine
\{ x, y ? 0 } ->
I can write that and use it no problem, no annotation ever needed
I can put it into the repl and it'll infer the most general type
Sure, but you just forced destructuring of the record, which may not be valid or wanted
fair, but that's unrelated to type inference
and there's always \({ y ? 0 } as rec) ->
if destructuring other fields isn't desired

I think the biggest problem here is that separating the value from the type makes little sense.

As in, seeing this would essentially never be correct/wanted and you would need to specify a default value all over the place:

Point : { x : F32, y : F32, z ? F32 }
There is a reason that in essentially all languages with the feature the default value is part of the type.
Rust is the exception due to how traits work. That wouldn't work for roc cause you would have to lock it behind an opaque type which would remove the convenience and purpose of default values.
hm, is it usually part of the types in structural types though?
that part definitely feels strange to me :sweat_smile:
Like the zig and c++ examples above?
I have seen that used all the time in zig for various configs.
Zig and C++ use nominal types for these, right?
like they don't have a concept of structural types for records/structs as far as I know
ah yeah, structural vs nominal...not struct vs classes and other object type things
yeah exactly
Trying to think about a better workaround....but I get the constraints....

Currently I am thinking that the best option for Roc might be:

  1. Rename them to default valued record fields (I think clarity here will avoid confusion of use case).
  2. Maybe add a syntax for access without destructuring? foo.bar ? 3... though realized that this may not truly be needed cause you can do:
x : { z ? Num a } -> Num a
x = \y ->
   {z ? 3} = y
   z

1. Maybe if needed, change away from ? if that feels too much like it is optional and not a default value. Maybe there is a clearer syntax in the type.
    It might be redundant, but it would probably be clearer if we included the default value both in the type and the actual source:
x : { z : Num a ? 3 } -> Num a
x = \{z ? 3} ->
   z

Or could go more explicit about defaults in the type:

x : { z : Num a = default } -> Num a
x = \{z ? 3} ->
   z
I guess maybe I should put this in it's own proposal or as an addend to #ideas > Optional Record Fields -> Default Valued Record Fields
yeah certainly changing the name is the easiest thing to try! :big_smile:

The only reason I suggested putting a default value in the type definition is because it is in one location versus having to add a default in one or more locations, however, as @Richard Feldman state, it breaks the ability to type ROC without the type annotation. If you assign it to a function, then it would probably still be in at most one location anyways.

x1 : { y: Num a, z : Num a = default } -> Num a
x1 = \record & { z = 2 } -> record.y + record.z
callX1 =
   x1 { y: 1}

x2 : { y: Num a, z: Num a = default } -> Num a
x2 = \{y, z = 2} -> y + z
callX2 =
   x2 { y: 1 }
Why not use assignment syntax = instead of ?
Sure, that would be fine. Though if you remove it from the type annotations completely there is no signal to a user that they can skip the field
Hence, the type definition z : Num a = default
I like that more than using ?
Oh, you just missed that in your example
Fixed
Yeah, I think = default though verbose is my favorite syntax currently (though a large struct might make me change my mind)

Also, once in the actual function, I don't mind ?. Cause it is kinda asking if you need to set the default value or not. That said, I am totally fine with another syntax. Just have to make it mesh well with record update syntax. You don't want it to look too similar unless it is a direct parity syntax. Like it could maybe be a direct parity as {record ? z: 2} for z having a default value of 2. Similar to {record & z: 2} setting z to a value of 2.

Or keep the ? for type, but use assignment = when destructuring is another option

x : { y: Num a, z ? Num a } -> Num a
x = \{ y, z = 2 } -> record.y + record.z
callX =
   x { y: 1}
personally I like to reserve x = blah to mean one thing everywhere, namely: x is now blah
as opposed to "usually it means that, except if you're in a record destructure, in which case it means this is the default value"
it's also why = doesn't appear in types
or in record assignments
basically = is so fundamental, I don't like overloading it

Good point. Just had another idea. ?= as a new operator instead of just ?

?= means default assignment just like != means not equal?

x : { y: Num a, z ?= Num a } -> Num a
x = \{ y, z ?= 2 } -> y + z
callX =
   x { y: 1}
I'm open to that, but to be honest it feels longer without being clearer :man_shrugging:
and ?= looks weird in the type to me, next to all the :s

How about ?: instead of ? just for the type and keep ?= when defaulting?

x: { y: Num a, z ?: Num a }
?= would make sense if we switched from : to = for record fields. (I know this was discussed previously.)
what's the problem with ?
Nothing, just throwing ideas at the wall

Personally, I think the only real issue with ? is in the type annotation. Specifically with what @Anton mentioned here: https://roc.zulipchat.com/#narrow/stream/304641-ideas/topic/Optional.20Record.20Fields.20-.3E.20Default.20Valued.20Record.20Fields/near/423192768

It leads to a wrong image in the type annotation that suggests optionality. As such, seeing:

{
    height : Pixels,
    width : Pixels,
    title ? Str,
    description ? Str,
}

Suggests:

  1. title and description are optional. So new users will expect they can use them like a Option Str. With Option defined as [Some a, None].
  2. As mentioned in other locations, ? really only make sense as a function argument. It doesn't make sense as a general type. That really isn't clear.
the problem is that they are optional, it's just that beginners' guesses about what "optional" means are sometimes incorrect :big_smile:
at the end of the day, if the goal is that you are able to omit fields, "those fields are optional" is an accurate way to describe them
I buy that it may turn out to be more helpful to present the feature using the word "default" rather than "optional" in the docs, to emphasize that having a default is required

This is definitely why I think what I mentioned at the top of this thread is much cleaner. It is a full system where they type definitoin specifies the default value. As such, you can fully remove the idea of optionality. Just any record can have default initialized fields.

If only I can figure out a way to make it decidable....
but at the end of the day, if the goal of the feature is to let you omit things, the most common term to describe "things that can be omitted" is "optional"
I don't think that part is fixable though
like because of English, not because of programming
we can use other words, and intentionally avoid saying "optional," to try to create a more accurate mental model
but at the end of the day we can't escape that "optional" is the word that describes these fields
no matter what the design is
I don't think that is true. No one thinks of C++ struct fields as optional. That said, they can have default values.
I get that we are a different language and that makes things more complex from a design perspective, but the model exist in other languages just fine.

Brendan Hansknecht said:

I don't think that is true. No one thinks of C++ struct fields as optional. That said, they can have default values.

well but in that case you don't "omit" anything, right? Default just means it's initialized to something as opposed to uninitialized
like normally in a Roc record you have to specify all the fields, and then when using this feature, you can omit some of them
I mean it looks identical to the roc example. So I would say that you "omit" just as much.
But yeah, the big difference is nominal vs structural types and having constructors (even if they default to just zero or no initialization)
for sure!

I think a clearer example is zig. They require initialization. They also have default value struct fields. As such, when looking at the individual functions, the record initialization look identical to what it looks like in roc.

In zig, there is no question about fields being optional. I don't think anyone would say that Foo here has an optional field a. Instead, users would say that a was initialized to a default value instead of specified by the user.

const Foo = extern struct {
    a: i32 = 1234,
    b: i32,
};

// Invalid: error: missing struct field: b
// export fn foo0() Foo {
//     return .{};
// }

export fn foo1() Foo {
    return .{.b = 7};
}

export fn foo2() Foo {
    return .{.a = 22, .b = 7};
}

From a design perspective, I would say that zig gets to have its cake and eat it too. Currently roc is not that clear or clean cut. It is not just a matter of calling the fields optional or default valued. The actual roc semantics are less useful than those in zig.

I get that this may be a fundamental constraint of structural types with a decidable type system, but that doesn't make me like it any more.

Also, it definitely isn't a fundamental constraint actually. As mentioned above, technically this could work if we just repeat certain information in both the type annotation and the the source code. Of course, that isn't a nice solution, so we would want something better.

That said, just like you require defining a type if someone needs abilities. I still think it would make a lot of sense to require adding a type annotation if someone needs default values.

I fundamentally don't see what the actual downside to the language would be. I am not talking anything theoretical. I mean what would be a real downside.

People don't just go around deleting all type annotations from a program. If a user never adds a type annotation, they will never use the feature. As such, I don't see any case where this would lead to issues.

Issue https://github.com/roc-lang/roc/issues/6532 is directly related to this conversation when dealing with default syntax.

To fix this open issue, it needs to use both : and ? when dealing with multiple instances of a record.
: is needed to rename the variable
? is needed to default the variable

Point a : { x: Frac a, y: Frac a, z ? Frac a }

distanceBetweenPoints : Point a, Point a -> Frac a
distanceBetweenPoints = \point1, point2 ->
    { x: x1, y: y1, z: z1 ? 0.0 } = point1
    { x: x2, y: y2, z: z2 ? 0.0 } = point2

I would think it should be the other way around:

Point a : { x: Frac a, y: Frac a, z ? Frac a }

distanceBetweenPoints : Point a, Point a -> Frac a
distanceBetweenPoints = \point1, point2 ->
    { x: x1, y: y1, z ? 0.0: z1 } = point1
    { x: x2, y: y2, z ? 0.0: z2 } = point2

Brendan Hansknecht said:

I fundamentally don't see what the actual downside to the language would be. I am not talking anything theoretical. I mean what would be a real downside.

one that comes to mind is that the same syntax means different things depending on whether it's a value or a type, which is normally fine because values and types don't appear next to each other, but e.g. these would all be valid:

{
    name : Str,
    type : Type ?= Str,
    email : Str,
}

:point_up: the middle Str is a tag named Str, not the builtin Str type

{
    name : Str,
    arity : List [One, Many] ?= [One, Many],
}

:point_up: the first [One, Many] is a tag union type, and the second [One, Many] is a list with 2 elements in it
Haha. That is definitely a good hole in that kind of syntax.....hmm

Would something like this work, so no destructuring is required?

distanceBetweenPoints = \point1 & { z: 0.0 }, point2 & { z: 0.0 } -> Frac a
    Num.sqrt (
            Num.pow (point2.x - point1.x) 2.0 +
            Num.pow (point2.y - point1.y) 2.0 +
            Num.pow (point2.z - point1.z) 2.0
    )

I think the two concerns with that syntax would be:

  1. it looks a lot like normal record update syntax which may be confusing.
  2. it leads to the same questions brought up in #ideas > reddit record update syntax. Like what if I try this or other more complicated variations:
zeroZ = { z: 0.0 }
distanceBetweenPoints = \point1 & zeroZ, point2 & zeroZ -> Frac a
    Num.sqrt (
            Num.pow (point2.x - point1.x) 2.0 +
            Num.pow (point2.y - point1.y) 2.0 +
            Num.pow (point2.z - point1.z) 2.0
    )

For the very first suggestion: What if you could have _ in the types to infere the default value (the actual = as a default value could be something else), just like you can use _ in other places in a type definition? You could delete the type def if you want to in that case and the type def wouldn't contain redundant info.

Point : { x: F32, y: F32, z: F32 = 0.0 }
# OR
Point : { x: F32, y: F32, z: F32 = _ }

If we go with something like this, another valid question would be: Would it be a good idea to allow the use of _ when the type is not directly above a function definition? If we did, it would be consistent, short, but possibly more confusing (for that the = default ma be better. If we don't, it creates incosistency in the lang "Compiler err: You can use _ above a fn, but not here". But, it would be obvious what = _ means when the implementation has that.

Here a list of how other language are doing this (generated by Chat GPT):
Sure, here's the updated list including Kotlin and Scala:

  1. Kotlin: Kotlin supports default parameters in a straightforward manner. If a value for a parameter is not provided, the default value is used.
fun greet(name: String = "World") {
    println("Hello, $name!")
}

greet()  // prints "Hello, World!"
greet("Alice")  // prints "Hello, Alice!"
  1. Scala: Scala also supports default parameters. If a value for a parameter is not provided, the default value is used.
def greet(name: String = "World"): Unit = {
    println(s"Hello, $name!")
}

greet()  // prints "Hello, World!"
greet("Alice")  // prints "Hello, Alice!"
  1. Python: Python supports default parameters in a straightforward manner. If a value for a parameter is not provided, the default value is used.
def greet(name="World"):
    print(f"Hello, {name}!")

greet()  // prints "Hello, World!"
greet("Alice")  // prints "Hello, Alice!"
  1. JavaScript (ES6 and later): JavaScript supports default parameters using the assignment (=) operator in the function definition.
function greet(name = "World") {
    console.log(`Hello, ${name}!`);
}

greet();  // prints "Hello, World!"
greet("Alice");  // prints "Hello, Alice!"
  1. PHP: PHP also supports default parameters. If a value for a parameter is not provided, the default value is used.
function greet($name = "World") {
    echo "Hello, $name!";
}

greet();  // prints "Hello, World!"
greet("Alice");  // prints "Hello, Alice!"
  1. Swift: Swift supports default parameters as well. If a value for a parameter is not provided, the default value is used.
func greet(name: String = "World") {
    print("Hello, \(name)!")
}

greet()  // prints "Hello, World!"
greet(name: "Alice")  // prints "Hello, Alice!"
  1. C#: C# supports optional parameters that can be used as default parameters.
public void Greet(string name = "World") {
    Console.WriteLine($"Hello, {name}!");
}

Greet();  // prints "Hello, World!"
Greet("Alice");  // prints "Hello, Alice!"
  1. Rust: In Rust, you can define functions with default parameters by using the Option type. Here's how you can define a function with default parameters in Rust:
fn greet(name: Option<&str>, age: Option<i32>) {
    let name = name.unwrap_or("World");
    let age = age.unwrap_or(20);
    println!("Hello, {}! You are {} years old.", name, age);
}

In this case, if you call greet(None, None), it will print "Hello, World! You are 20 years old.". If you provide an argument, like greet(Some("Alice"), Some(30)), it will print "Hello, Alice! You are 30 years old.".

However, Rust does not support named arguments like Kotlin and Scala. You have to pass the arguments in the order they are defined in the function signature.

These are just a few examples. Many other languages also support this feature.

:thinking: , a way to combine the incoming record with the default and assigning it to a variable in the function signature would be neat, but thinking of a simple syntax to accomplish that is challenging. The syntax would have to mean, take the incoming data, join the default, and assign it to this variable name. Otherwise, we just stick with renaming the destrucutred data and default the values.

distanceBetweenPoints = \point1 : { & z: 0 }, point2 : { & z: 0 } -> Frac a
    Num.sqrt (
            Num.pow (point2.x - point1.x) 2.0 +
            Num.pow (point2.y - point1.y) 2.0 +
            Num.pow (point2.z - point1.z) 2.0
    )

distanceBetweenPoints = \{ x: x1, y: y1, z ? 0.0 : z1 }, { x: x2, y: y2, z ? 0.0 : z2 } -> Frac a
    Num.sqrt (
            Num.pow (x2 - x1) 2.0 +
            Num.pow (y2 - y1) 2.0 +
            Num.pow (z2 - z1) 2.0
    )

:thinking: would it make more sense to flip the pattern name and new variable on the colon in case of destruction?
So the direction would follow the normal variable assignment:

distanceBetweenPoints = \{ x1 :  x,  y1 :  y, z1 :  z ? 0.0 }, { x2 :  x,  y2:  y ,  z2 :  z ? 0.0  } -> Frac a
    Num.sqrt (
            Num.pow (x2 - x1) 2.0 +
            Num.pow (y2 - y1) 2.0 +
            Num.pow (z2 - z1) 2.0
    )

Alternative using as could work:

distanceBetweenPoints = \{ x as x1,  y  as y1, z  as  z1 ? 0.0 }, { x as  x2,  y as  y2 ,  z as  z2 ? 0.0  } -> Frac a
    Num.sqrt (
            Num.pow (x2 - x1) 2.0 +
            Num.pow (y2 - y1) 2.0 +
            Num.pow (z2 - z1) 2.0
    )
@Alf Richter Destructuring already uses the syntax { x: x1, y: y1 } for renaming, it just doesn't work with defaults yet.

Brian Teague said:

Alf Richter Destructuring already uses the syntax { x: x1, y: y1 } for renaming, it just doesn't work with defaults yet.

I know, but while thinking about the default value, the deconstruction and binding order (or using the colon here) feels a little bit strange. Using as could help to distinct between assignment and aliasing, and so provide a good starting point for the default values placement :thinking:

Richard Feldman said:

{
    name : Str,
    type : Type ?= Str,
    email : Str,
}

:point_up: the middle Str is a tag named Str, not the builtin Str type

I definitely don't like that as an outcome.

I definitely agree with the sentiment that default values _are_ optional, but am not as concerned about newcomers mixing them up with Optional/Maybe style types. We could mitigate that in the tutorial by just introducing default values before we introduce tag unions, so that the reader has already learned that 5 could be a meaningful value.

If the user then wants the "not present" meaning of optional, they could just do { x ? NotPresent } and the compiler would ensure everything is handled consistently and safely.
All that said, I do think ? in the function annotation syntax is fine (i.e. i prefer the exact annotation syntax we already have).

Regarding destructuring syntax, I'd be open to = or ?= in the destructuring syntax, though I don't have a strong opinion.

That said, stylistically, I'd prefer spacing on both sides of the ?, i.e. {title ? "oak"} instead of {title? "oak"}. The present style makes ? look like a part of the field's name (which it's not) rather than a relationship between the field and its value... and the spacing style for destructuring differs unnecessarily from that of the annotation.

What I am worried about is mixing value notions into a type definition, because values and types are distinct in Roc. If we're going to bend that, then why not just implement full dependent type support? It's a slippery slope.

Additionally, I'm worried about the data modeling abuse that would inevitably come from being able to specify defaults within a named type. We shouldn't have a Point with an optional z. We should have Point2D and Point3D instead.

iow, I'd feel better if Roc disallowed optional fields to be used in named types, limiting their use only to unnamed types used in specific contexts (like input to a function call).

I'd feel even better if default/optional values were a value level concept rather than also being a type-level concept, if that could be done while still having the default value feature without harming or special-casing this aspect of the type system.

iow, something like:

add : { x : Num a, y : Num a } -> Num a
add = \{ x, y ? 1 } -> x + y

five = add { x: 4 }

I'm guessing, but I suspect that with today's syntax and semantics, the Roc compiler sees a call site like is used to define five above, and supplies an { x : Num * } to the function, thus requiring the type system to know about optional fields.

As mentioned above, I believe optional fields are bad for type modeling, and so my proposal would be that the compiler instead have the call site take default values from the function definition, thus desugaring the last line in the above snippet to:

five = add { x: 4, y: 1}

Thus we can have present-day optional/default field behavior without type system support.

I was looking at Need to be able to default destructured values that are renamed., but saw it was linked here. The GitHub issues seems pretty straight forward to me, its just a combination of two existing features. However, I could see that syntax changing based on the results of this discussion. Does anyone have any guidance if I should start working on this bug?

I think we should support the syntax as described in the issue, e.g. { z: z1 ? 0.0 }.

I think it's mostly an update to the parser.
Ok, thanks! I'll start working on that.

I think we could add a test in https://github.com/roc-lang/roc/tree/main/crates/compiler/test_syntax/tests/snapshots/pass.

I don't think we have any tests currently for either renaming or optional record de-structure, so adding one that includes each of those too would be helpful I think.

Here is a similar test record_destructure_def.expr.roc
Oh, nice! I had started writing some tests somewhere else, so I'll move them over to that spot. Thanks for that!

I looked at this a little before and I think it is a bit more than just parser. I believe it requires some data structure unification to support 2 things at once. Instead of seeing them as two distinct things.

That said, it will start with the parser

Yeah, I've been digging into what nodes the AST would have, and I haven't found anything that is obviously correct. Its either a layer of nesting more than the rename variable/default value, or its a new variant in RecordField
Line 502 crates/compiler/parse/src/pattern.rs is the root of this parsing
You can see it handles ? or : but it need to be able to handle both
Can be just :, just ? or : ... ?

So, one thought I had was switching the order of the syntax. Instead of doing {x: x1 ? 0.0}, the syntax could be {x ? 0.0 : x1}. This seems clearer to me, as you could read it as 'the field x or 0.0, is equal to x1'. I'm a little iffy on whether there should be whitespace before the : or not.

One downside I thought of is that it happens to visually be the same syntax as the ternary operator in languages like javascript. Not sure how big of an issue that is though
Yeah, it is a weird syntax no matter what.
I think avoiding ternary is a good idea to avoid confusion
x: x1 ? 0.0 -> x aliased as x1 with default value 0.0
The main reason I thought the original was better was just how everything would line up nicely both inline and multi-line, and that appealed to my ocd
Fair points! I'll keep on that path then. I just wanted to make sure that things were thought through
I think the ternary syntax makes sense here; but for the reasons above I don't think it's the best choice. But that's just my 2c
